text:
The Kaijinmaru (海神丸, Kaijinmaru) Hunter × Hunter - Episode 2 (1999) is a passenger ship used to transport prospective Hunter Exam applicants Hunter × Hunter - Volume 1, Chapter 1 to Dolle Harbor. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 1, Chapter 3 

Crew

Captain
Katzo

Plot

Hunter Exam arc

The Kaijinmaru stations at Whale Island and picks up applicants of the 287th Hunter Exam, including a boy by the name of Gon Freecss. On the night of the voyage, the Kaijinmaru gets caught in a freak storm and tumultuous waves that shifts and turns the ship, but the Captain manages to navigate through it. The following morning, he calls the prior night's experience a fair toss-up, and when he sees the passengers in such a disdainful state, he disparages them. However, he sees that three passengers (including Gon) survived the rough storm and compliments them. On the ship's deck, the Captain talks with Gon, who has a presentiment of a greater storm than the last on the horizon. Delighted to hear about the boy's background and his astute instincts, the Captain takes the boy to the deck and makes an announcement to the ship's passengers about a greater storm on its way. Most passengers jumped out of the ship on the lifeboats, save for the three passengers who impressed the Captain. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 1, Chapter 2 

The Captain then interviews the remaining three, and they introduce themselves as Gon, Leorio, and Kurapika. In the interview, the Captain reveals that the Qualification Exam has already begun. After an altercation between Leorio and Kurapika, the two go on deck amid the second storm to duel one another. The duel is canceled after Gon tries to rescue a man named Katzo, but he himself is nearly thrown overboard, so Leorio and Kurapika rescue the boy from danger. Impressed with the trio, the Captain passes them and takes them to the next location of the Qualification Exam. 

The following day, the Kaijinmaru docks at Dolle Harbor and drops off its trio of hopeful Hunters. 

Trivia

The name of the Kaijinmaru literally translates to "sea god" (海神, Kaijin–lit. "Sea God"), where "-maru" (丸) is a common suffix for Japanese ship names used typically for merchant ships.

Manga and Anime Differences

The ship is only named in the 1999 adaptation.
